slides.label: Overview
slides.title: Authentication Flow
slides.prose:
When a user signs in to Emberflow — via OAuth, magic link, or the CLI device flow — a coordinated exchange happens between six components.

Each step is designed to be stateless on the server side. The client never sees raw credentials; only a signed session token.

Navigate through each step to see how the pieces connect.

slides.label: Step 1
slides.title: Sign-in Request
slides.prose:
The client sends a POST to /api/auth/sign-in/social with the chosen provider and a callback URL.

This works the same whether the user is in a browser clicking "Sign in with Google" or using the CLI's device code flow.

The server never renders a sign-in form — it returns a redirect URL that the client follows.

slides.label: Step 2
slides.title: Auth Delegation
slides.prose:
The Express server doesn't handle auth logic itself. It delegates to better-auth, which manages provider configuration, PKCE challenges, and callback validation.

This separation means the server routes stay thin — auth is a pluggable service, not wired into the request handlers.

slides.label: Step 3
slides.title: OAuth Exchange
slides.prose:
The auth service redirects the user to the external provider (Google or GitHub). The provider authenticates the user and returns an authorization code.

The auth service exchanges this code for an access token and retrieves the user's profile — email, name, and avatar.

  • Google: OpenID Connect flow
  • GitHub: OAuth 2.0 authorization code
  • Magic link: email verification (no provider)

slides.label: Step 4
slides.title: User Persistence
slides.prose:
With a verified identity, the auth service upserts the user in PostgreSQL — creating a new record on first sign-in, or linking to an existing account by email.

This is also where account linking happens: if a user signed up with Google but later signs in with GitHub using the same email, the accounts merge.
